questions.question: Your team is starting a new sprint with a mix of new features and bug fixes. The PM asks you to create a test plan. What should you prioritize first?

options.text: Write automated tests for all new features before the sprint starts, ensuring full coverage from day one.
Identify the highest-risk areas based on user impact and code complexity, then allocate testing effort proportionally to risk.
Test everything equally — each feature and bug fix gets the same amount of testing time to ensure fairness.
Focus exclusively on regression testing since existing features are more important than new ones.

questions.explanation: Risk-based testing prioritizes effort where failures would have the greatest impact, maximizing test value within time constraints.

questions.question: A payment processing module was recently refactored. No other modules were changed. Where should you focus your testing?

options.text: Test only the payment module since that's what changed — testing unchanged code wastes time.
Run the full test suite across all modules equally since any code change could introduce bugs anywhere.
Focus heavily on the payment module AND its integration points — checkout flow, refund process, receipt generation — since dependencies may break.
Skip testing the payment module since refactoring shouldn't change behavior, and focus on new features instead.

questions.explanation: Refactored modules plus their integration points are highest risk — the module itself and anything that depends on it could break.

questions.question: Your test suite has 95% code coverage but a critical bug slipped to production. The team asks how this happened. What's the most likely explanation?

options.text: Code coverage measures lines executed, not scenarios validated. Tests may have hit the code without asserting correct behavior.
95% isn't high enough — you need 100% coverage to prevent all bugs from reaching production.
The coverage tool miscounted and actual coverage was lower than reported.
Code coverage only works for unit tests. The bug was in integration logic which coverage tools can't measure.

questions.explanation: Code coverage measures execution, not validation — tests can execute code without meaningful assertions, giving false confidence.

questions.question: After fixing a login bug, QA finds the 'forgot password' flow is now broken. What type of testing should have caught this?

options.text: Smoke testing — a quick check of major features would have surfaced this before deeper testing.
Regression testing — verifying that the bug fix didn't break related functionality in the authentication module.
Acceptance testing — the product owner should validate all user stories after each fix.
Performance testing — the broken flow is likely caused by increased latency from the fix.

questions.explanation: Regression testing specifically checks that code changes don't break existing functionality in related areas.

questions.question: You have 3 days to test a release with 20 features. Full regression takes 5 days. How do you approach this?

options.text: Run full regression anyway and delay the release — shipping untested code is never acceptable.
Skip regression entirely and only test the 20 new features since those are the only changes.
Prioritize new features by risk, run automated regression for core flows, and do targeted manual regression on areas affected by changes.
Split the team: half tests new features, half runs regression. Both finish in 3 days with parallel effort.

questions.explanation: Combining risk-based prioritization with automated regression for core paths maximizes coverage within time constraints.

questions.question: The team is shipping a feature that integrates with a third-party payment API. During testing, the API works fine. What risk are you overlooking?

options.text: The API might change its response format or go down in production. You should test error handling, timeouts, and degraded responses using mocks.
Third-party APIs are guaranteed by SLA, so if it works in testing it will work in production.
The risk is purely contractual — if the API fails, the vendor is liable, not your team.
You should avoid third-party APIs entirely and build the payment system in-house to eliminate the risk.

questions.explanation: Third-party dependencies can fail, change, or degrade — testing must include error paths, timeouts, and fallback behavior using mocked failure scenarios.

questions.question: You're testing an e-commerce checkout. There are 3 payment methods, 2 shipping options, and 2 user types (guest/registered). Testing all combinations is 12 scenarios. The PM wants it done in 2 hours. Best approach?

options.text: Test only the most common combination (credit card + standard shipping + registered user) since that covers 80% of real traffic.
Use pairwise testing to cover all pairs of variables in fewer scenarios — typically 6-8 tests catch most interaction bugs.
Randomly pick 4 combinations and hope they catch any issues — random sampling is statistically valid.
Automate all 12 combinations immediately — even though setup takes 2 hours, it saves time in future sprints.

questions.explanation: Pairwise testing ensures every pair of variable values is tested at least once, catching most interaction bugs with significantly fewer test cases.

questions.question: Your regression suite has grown to 2,000 tests and takes 4 hours. The team complains it slows down releases. What's the best long-term solution?

options.text: Delete the oldest tests since they cover legacy features that rarely change.
Categorize tests into tiers: fast critical-path tests run on every PR, full suite runs nightly. Optimize slow tests and remove redundant ones.
Switch entirely to manual testing — human testers can cover more scenarios faster than slow automated suites.
Only run tests that correspond to changed files using git diff — unchanged code doesn't need retesting.

questions.explanation: Tiered test execution balances speed and coverage — fast feedback on PRs with comprehensive nightly runs catches regressions without blocking releases.

questions.question: You join a project mid-sprint. There's no test plan, no documented test cases, and the release is in 5 days. What do you do first?

options.text: Refuse to test until proper documentation exists — testing without a plan is unprofessional and risky.
Start writing a comprehensive test plan covering all features from scratch before executing any tests.
Talk to developers and PMs to identify highest-risk changes, then create a lightweight risk-based test plan focused on critical paths.
Start exploratory testing immediately without any planning — action is more important than documentation under time pressure.

questions.explanation: Under time pressure, a lightweight risk-based approach ensures critical areas are covered while avoiding analysis paralysis or untargeted testing.

questions.question: Your app handles financial data. A new feature adds CSV export. The developer says it's low risk — just formatting. You disagree. Why?

options.text: CSV files can contain formula injection (=CMD) that executes when opened in Excel. Financial data exports also risk exposing PII if access controls aren't applied to the export endpoint.
CSV formatting is complex and error-prone — special characters, Unicode, and large files frequently cause parsing bugs that corrupt data.
Exports bypass the UI layer, so any validation that exists only in the frontend won't apply to exported data.
The export feature adds server load that could degrade performance for other users during peak hours.

questions.explanation: CSV injection and unauthorized data exposure are security risks that make any data export feature high-risk, especially with financial or personal data.

questions.question: Your team achieves 90% branch coverage but mutation testing shows only 40% mutation score. What does this reveal about your test suite?

options.text: Branch coverage and mutation score measure different things — 40% mutation score means 60% of code mutations went undetected, indicating weak assertions despite high execution coverage.
Mutation testing tools are unreliable with high branch coverage — the 40% score is likely a false negative from equivalent mutants.
The discrepancy means the coverage tool is miscounting branches. Recalibrate the coverage tool to align with mutation results.
90% branch coverage is already excellent. Mutation score below 50% is normal and acceptable for production code.

questions.explanation: Low mutation score with high coverage reveals tests that execute code without verifying correctness — assertions are too weak or missing.

questions.question: After a database migration, all automated regression tests pass, but users report data inconsistencies in production. What did the regression suite miss?

options.text: Regression tests ran against a clean test database, not migrated data. They didn't test the migration path — existing data transformations, edge cases in legacy records, and data integrity post-migration.
Automated tests can't detect data issues. Only manual inspection of database records can validate data correctness after migrations.
The tests passed because the migration was rolled back automatically in the test environment, so tests ran against pre-migration data.
Database migrations are infrastructure changes, not application changes, so they fall outside the scope of regression testing.

questions.explanation: Regression tests on clean data miss migration-specific issues — tests must also run against representative migrated data to catch transformation errors.

questions.question: You're exploratory testing a new search feature. After 30 minutes you've only tested basic keyword searches. A senior tester says you're not being exploratory enough. What should you do differently?

options.text: Write formal test cases first, then execute them systematically — exploratory testing needs structure.
Vary your inputs and paths: try special characters, empty searches, very long queries, search-then-filter combos, and observe how results behave under different conditions.
Speed up by testing more keywords faster — the more searches you try, the more exploratory it becomes.
Switch to automated testing since manual testing can't adequately cover search functionality.

questions.explanation: Exploratory testing means actively varying inputs, paths, and conditions to discover unexpected behavior — not just repeating similar scenarios.

questions.question: During usability testing, a user completes a task but says 'That was confusing.' The feature works correctly. Should you file a bug?

options.text: No — the feature works as designed. Confusion is a training issue, not a bug.
Yes — usability issues are valid bugs. If users find the workflow confusing, the design needs improvement even if the code is correct.
No — file a feature request instead, since the current implementation meets requirements.
Yes — but only if multiple users report the same confusion. One user's opinion isn't enough data.

questions.explanation: Usability issues are bugs — software that confuses users fails its purpose regardless of whether the code functions correctly.

questions.question: A form field accepts ages 18-65. Which values should you test for boundary analysis?

options.text: Test 18 and 65 only — those are the boundary values.
Test 17, 18, 19, 64, 65, 66 — values at, just below, and just above each boundary to catch off-by-one errors.
Test 1, 18, 40, 65, 100 — a spread across the full range gives better coverage.
Test 0 and 999 — extreme values are more likely to find bugs than boundary values.

questions.explanation: Boundary value analysis tests at, below, and above each boundary — off-by-one errors (using < instead of <=) are among the most common bugs.

questions.question: A new build is deployed to the staging environment. Before starting detailed testing, what should you do first?

options.text: Run a smoke test — verify core flows like login, main navigation, and key transactions work. If smoke fails, reject the build immediately.
Start regression testing from the beginning of the test suite to find issues in order.
Check the deployment logs for errors — if no errors in logs, the build is stable enough for testing.
Wait for the automated test suite to complete before touching the build manually.

questions.explanation: Smoke testing quickly verifies basic functionality before investing time in detailed testing — it catches build-breaking issues early.

questions.question: You're exploratory testing an e-commerce app and notice the cart total shows $0.00 when you add an item priced at $0.005. The price displays as '$0.01' on the product page. What's happening?

options.text: The product page rounds up for display, but the cart truncates to 2 decimal places instead of rounding, causing a floating-point display inconsistency.
The cart correctly shows $0.00 because items under $0.01 are free — this is standard e-commerce behavior for fractional cents.
The product page and cart use different currency formatting libraries that handle sub-cent amounts differently.
This is a floating-point arithmetic error. JavaScript can't represent $0.005 exactly, and the cart calculation loses precision.

questions.explanation: Inconsistent rounding between display contexts is a common bug — the product page and cart should use the same rounding strategy for prices.

questions.question: A form has 15 required fields. After filling them all and clicking Submit, the page reloads with a validation error on field 3 — but fields 4-15 are now empty. What usability issue should you report?

options.text: The validation should happen inline as the user fills each field, not on submit. And submitted data must persist across validation failures so users don't re-enter 12 fields.
The form should auto-save to local storage every 5 seconds so data is never lost.
15 fields is too many — the form should be split into multiple pages regardless of the validation behavior.
The Submit button should be disabled until all fields pass validation, preventing the reload entirely.

questions.explanation: Two issues: validation should be inline (immediate feedback) and form data must persist on validation failure — losing user input is a critical usability bug.

questions.question: A text field has a 255-character limit. You paste 300 characters and submit — the form accepts it silently but the database truncates to 255. No error shown. What type of bug is this?

options.text: It's not a bug — silent truncation is standard behavior for databases with character limits.
Data integrity bug. The application should validate input length before submission and show an error, or clearly indicate truncation occurred. Silent data loss is unacceptable.
Database configuration issue — the column should be TEXT type with no limit instead of VARCHAR(255).
Frontend bug only — add a maxlength attribute to the HTML input and the problem is solved.

questions.explanation: Silent data loss is a serious bug — validation should prevent oversized input or clearly warn the user that content will be truncated.

questions.question: Your smoke test suite has grown from 10 to 80 tests and now takes 45 minutes. The team says it's no longer useful as a smoke test. Why?

options.text: Smoke tests should be fast (5-10 min) and cover only critical paths. At 80 tests and 45 minutes, it's become a regression suite — trim it back to core functionality checks.
80 tests is fine — smoke tests should be comprehensive. The problem is test execution speed, not test count.
Run the 80 tests in parallel to bring execution time under 10 minutes, keeping full coverage.
Convert all 80 to automated tests. Manual smoke tests shouldn't exceed 10 cases, but automated suites can be any size.

questions.explanation: Smoke tests are meant to be quick sanity checks of critical paths — if they take too long, they lose their purpose as a fast build-validation gate.

questions.question: During exploratory testing of a messaging app, you notice that sending a message with only whitespace (spaces and tabs) creates a visible empty chat bubble. Should you report this?

options.text: No — whitespace is valid text input. The app correctly displays what the user sent.
Yes — the app should either trim whitespace and prevent empty sends, or not render empty-looking bubbles. This creates confusing UX and could be exploited to spam blank messages.
No — this is an edge case that real users would never trigger intentionally.
Yes — but only as a low-priority cosmetic issue since it doesn't affect functionality.

questions.explanation: Whitespace-only messages create confusing empty bubbles and potential spam — the app should validate message content, not just message presence.

questions.question: You're testing an accessibility feature. A screen reader announces a button as 'Button' with no label. Sighted users see 'Submit Order' on screen. What's the root cause?

options.text: The button text is rendered as a background image or CSS pseudo-element, not actual text content. The button needs an aria-label or visible text node for screen readers.
The screen reader software is outdated and can't parse modern HTML button elements.
The button uses JavaScript to dynamically set text after page load, and screen readers only read initial HTML content.
The button's role attribute is set incorrectly, overriding the visible text for assistive technology.

questions.explanation: Text rendered via CSS/images is invisible to screen readers — accessible buttons need text content in the DOM or an aria-label attribute.

questions.question: A date picker allows selecting dates from Jan 1, 2020 to Dec 31, 2025. You select Feb 29, 2024 and it works. You select Feb 29, 2023 — the form submits without error but saves as Mar 1, 2023. What type of defect is this?

options.text: Silent date overflow — the backend accepts the invalid date and rolls it forward instead of rejecting it. Validation must catch non-existent dates like Feb 29 in non-leap years before saving.
Frontend validation gap — the date picker UI should prevent selection of Feb 29 in non-leap years.
This is correct behavior — most date libraries auto-correct invalid dates to the nearest valid date.
Timezone issue — the server in a different timezone interprets Feb 29 differently than the client.

questions.explanation: Silent date overflow is a data integrity bug — invalid dates must be rejected, not silently corrected, as auto-correction produces unexpected stored data.

questions.question: Smoke tests pass on a new build, but 30% of regression tests fail. The failures are all in the reporting module. What does this tell you about your smoke test design?

options.text: Smoke tests are working correctly — they test core flows, and reporting isn't core.
The smoke suite has a coverage gap — it lacks any checks on the reporting module. A good smoke suite should include at least one representative test from each critical module.
This proves smoke tests are unreliable and the team should skip them and run full regression every time.
Reporting failures are likely caused by test data issues, not real bugs — the smoke suite correctly ignored data-dependent modules.

questions.explanation: A well-designed smoke suite should have minimal but representative coverage of all critical modules — missing an entire module indicates a gap in smoke test design.

questions.question: Your team is deciding between Selenium and Playwright for a new web automation project. The app uses modern React with dynamic content loading. Which factor matters most?

options.text: Selenium is older and more widely supported, so it's always the safer choice for any web project.
Playwright has built-in auto-waiting for dynamic elements and better handles modern SPA frameworks, reducing flaky tests from timing issues.
Both tools are identical in capability — the choice should be based purely on which language your team prefers.
Neither works well with React — you should use React Testing Library exclusively since it's built specifically for React apps.

questions.explanation: Playwright's auto-waiting and modern architecture handle dynamic SPAs better out of the box, reducing the flakiness common with explicit waits in Selenium.

questions.question: Your automated test uses the selector `div.container > div:nth-child(3) > span.price-text` to find a price element. After a minor UI redesign, the test breaks. What's the underlying problem?

options.text: The selector is too brittle — it depends on DOM structure and CSS classes that change with redesigns. Use a data-testid attribute instead.
CSS selectors can't handle dynamic content. Switch to XPath for more resilient element location.
The test should use coordinates (x,y) to click elements since visual position is more stable than DOM structure.
The selector is fine — the test correctly caught a breaking UI change that should be investigated.

questions.explanation: Structural CSS selectors are fragile — data-testid attributes provide stable, intent-based selectors that survive UI refactors.

questions.question: Automated tests pass on your local machine but fail in CI with 'element not found' errors. The application code is identical. Most likely cause?

options.text: CI runners are slower with less CPU/memory, so pages load slower. Tests hit timeouts waiting for elements that appeared instantly on your fast local machine.
CI uses a different browser version that doesn't support the CSS selectors in your tests.
Your tests depend on local data or cookies that don't exist in the clean CI environment.
CI runs tests in alphabetical order while locally they run in the order you wrote them, causing dependency issues.

questions.explanation: CI environments typically have fewer resources, causing slower page loads — tests need proper waits and timeouts rather than relying on local machine speed.

questions.question: Your automation suite has 500 tests. After a login page redesign, 200 tests fail. What design pattern would have prevented this mass failure?

options.text: Page Object Model — login logic in one class. UI change = update one file, not 200 tests.
Record-and-playback — re-record the login flow once and replay across all tests.
Data-driven testing — parameterize login credentials so tests adapt to UI changes.
Keyword-driven testing — abstract login into a keyword that auto-adjusts to any UI.

questions.explanation: Page Object Model centralizes page interactions — when the login UI changes, you update one page object instead of hundreds of individual tests.

questions.question: Your test framework uses a global setup that creates a test user, runs all tests, then deletes the user. Test A modifies the user's profile, causing Test B (which runs after) to fail. What's wrong with this approach?

options.text: Tests share mutable state through the global user. Each test should create its own isolated test data and clean up after itself to avoid inter-test dependencies.
Test A should restore the user profile to its original state in a teardown step. Shared setup is efficient and correct.
Run tests in a fixed order so Test B always runs before Test A. Test ordering solves state dependencies.
The global setup should create multiple users — one per test — but still in the global setup for efficiency.

questions.explanation: Shared mutable state causes test coupling — isolated test data ensures tests are independent, repeatable, and can run in any order.

questions.question: You need to test a dropdown that loads options dynamically from an API. Your test clicks the dropdown, but the assertion fails because options haven't loaded yet. What's the correct fix?

options.text: Add a 3-second sleep after clicking the dropdown to give the API time to respond.
Wait for the specific option element to appear in the DOM using an explicit wait condition, then proceed with the assertion.
Mock the API to return options instantly, eliminating the loading delay entirely.
Increase the global implicit wait timeout to 30 seconds so all element lookups wait longer.

questions.explanation: Explicit waits on specific conditions are the correct approach — sleeps are brittle and slow, implicit waits apply globally and mask real issues.

questions.question: Your CI pipeline runs 300 UI tests sequentially, taking 45 minutes. What's the most effective way to reduce this without removing tests?

options.text: Upgrade the CI runner to a faster machine — more CPU and memory will proportionally reduce test execution time.
Parallelize tests across multiple containers or shards. Split the 300 tests into groups that run simultaneously.
Convert all UI tests to API tests, which are inherently faster.
Run tests in headless mode — removing the browser UI makes tests 10x faster.

questions.explanation: Parallelization across multiple containers is the most effective way to reduce total execution time — 300 tests split into 6 shards of 50 cuts time by ~6x.

questions.question: A test that passed for 6 months suddenly starts failing intermittently — it passes on retry. No code changes were made. What should you investigate?

options.text: Delete and rewrite the test — after 6 months, test code degrades and needs refreshing.

Check for race conditions, timing dependencies, or external service flakiness. Intermittent failures usually indicate the test relies on something non-deterministic like timing, network, or test order.
Add automatic retry logic and move on — if it passes on retry, the underlying issue doesn't matter.
The CI infrastructure is degrading. Request a new build server to resolve intermittent hardware-related failures.

questions.explanation: Intermittent failures point to non-deterministic dependencies — timing, external services, or shared state. Retries mask root causes; investigate and fix.

questions.question: Your team wants to add API testing alongside existing UI tests. The UI tests use Playwright. Should you use Playwright for API tests too?

options.text: Yes — using one framework simplifies maintenance and training. Playwright supports API testing via request context.
No — always use dedicated API tools like Postman for API testing since Playwright is a browser tool.
It depends. For API tests tied to UI flows, Playwright works well. For standalone API contract/load testing, dedicated tools like REST Assured or k6 may be more appropriate.
No — mixing test types in one framework creates confusion. API and UI tests should always be in separate repositories.

questions.explanation: Tool choice depends on context — Playwright handles API calls within UI test flows, but dedicated API tools offer richer features for standalone API testing.

questions.question: Your test needs to verify that a dynamically generated table has exactly 50 rows after filtering. The table uses virtual scrolling — only 10 rows are in the DOM at a time. How do you verify the count?

options.text: Scroll through the entire table programmatically, counting rows as they render, until you reach the end.
Assert against the displayed row count indicator in the UI (e.g., 'Showing 1-10 of 50') or query the underlying API/data source directly instead of counting DOM elements.
Set the virtual scroll viewport height to a very large value so all 50 rows render at once, then count them.
Use JavaScript to query the component's internal state for the total row count, bypassing the DOM entirely.

questions.explanation: With virtual scrolling, DOM elements don't reflect total data — verify via UI count indicators or API response, not DOM element counting.

questions.question: Your team has 3 flaky tests that fail ~10% of the time. The CI pipeline reruns failed tests up to 3 times. All 3 eventually pass on retry. What's the hidden risk?

options.text: Retries mask real bugs. A test that fails 10% of the time might be detecting a genuine race condition in application code that occurs under specific timing. You're shipping a bug by retrying past it.
Retrying costs compute time. Three flaky tests with 3 retries adds 9 extra test runs, significantly increasing CI cost.
No real risk — if the test passes on retry, the failure was environmental and the code is fine.
The risk is test suite credibility. Developers will start ignoring all failures, even real ones, because 'it's probably flaky.'

questions.explanation: Flaky tests may detect real intermittent bugs — automatic retries hide genuine race conditions or timing-dependent defects that affect users in production.

questions.question: Your automation suite grew from 100 to 1,000 tests over 2 years. Test maintenance now consumes 40% of QA time. What's the most likely architectural problem?

options.text: Too many tests. Prune back to 500 and only automate critical paths.
Poor abstraction layers — tests likely duplicate setup logic, use brittle selectors, and lack proper page objects or helper functions. Refactor shared patterns into reusable components.
The test framework is outdated. Migrate to a newer framework with better auto-healing capabilities.
1,000 tests is the natural limit for maintainability. Split into two separate test suites maintained by different teams.

questions.explanation: High maintenance cost usually stems from poor abstraction — duplicated logic, brittle selectors, and missing patterns make every change ripple across many tests.

questions.question: You send a POST request to create a user and receive a 201 status code. Your test only checks the status code. What's missing?

options.text: Nothing — 201 confirms successful creation. Status codes are the definitive indicator of API behavior.
Validate the response body (returned user data matches input), response headers (Content-Type, Location), and verify the user actually exists with a follow-up GET request.
Check response time — if the API took too long, the creation might have partially failed.
Verify the database directly — API responses can lie about creation status.

questions.explanation: Status code alone is insufficient — verify response body content, headers, and actual resource creation to ensure the API behaves correctly end-to-end.

questions.question: The frontend team reports their app broke after a backend deployment. The API now returns `user_name` instead of `userName`. What testing practice would have caught this?

options.text: Contract testing — validating that the API response structure matches the agreed schema (field names, types, required fields) would catch naming changes before deployment.
Load testing — the naming change would surface under high request volume.
End-to-end testing — running the full app with frontend and backend together would catch integration issues.
Unit testing on the backend — a unit test for the serializer would prevent field name changes.

questions.explanation: Contract testing explicitly validates API response structure against an agreed schema, catching breaking changes like renamed fields before they reach consumers.

questions.question: An API endpoint returns user profiles. It responds in 50ms for a single user but takes 8 seconds when returning 1,000 users. What should you test?

options.text: Nothing abnormal — linear scaling (50ms x 1000 = 50 seconds) means 8 seconds is actually faster than expected.
Test pagination behavior. The API should support pagination to avoid returning massive datasets. Also test response time with various page sizes to find the optimal limit.
Only test the single-user endpoint since that's the primary use case. The 1,000-user scenario is unrealistic.
Increase the server timeout to 30 seconds to accommodate large responses.

questions.explanation: APIs returning large datasets should implement pagination — test that pagination works correctly and that response times remain acceptable at various page sizes.

questions.question: You're testing an API that requires authentication. You send a request without an auth token and receive a 200 OK with user data. What does this indicate?

options.text: The endpoint is intentionally public — some user data is meant to be publicly accessible.
Critical security bug — the endpoint is missing authentication middleware. Unauthenticated requests should receive 401 Unauthorized, not 200 with data.
The API has implicit authentication via IP allowlisting, so tokens aren't required from known networks.
The test environment has authentication disabled for convenience. This wouldn't happen in production.

questions.explanation: An endpoint returning data without authentication is a critical security vulnerability — missing auth middleware exposes user data to anyone.

questions.question: You send a DELETE request to /api/users/999 (non-existent user). The API returns 200 OK. Is this a bug?

options.text: No — DELETE is idempotent. Whether the resource existed or not, the end state is the same: it doesn't exist. 200 is acceptable.
Yes — the API should return 404 Not Found. The client asked to delete a specific resource that doesn't exist, and the API should communicate that.
It depends on API design philosophy. Both 200 and 404 are valid for DELETE on non-existent resources — check the API specification for the intended behavior.
Yes — it should return 204 No Content since nothing was deleted, which is different from 200 OK.

questions.explanation: DELETE on non-existent resources is a design decision — some APIs return 404 (resource-centric), others 200/204 (idempotent). The API spec defines correct behavior.

questions.question: Your API returns a `created_at` field. Sometimes it's `2024-01-15T10:30:00Z` and sometimes `1705312200` (Unix timestamp). What type of contract violation is this?

options.text: Inconsistent type contract — the same field returns different data types (string vs number) depending on the code path, breaking consumers that expect a consistent format.
Not a violation — both represent the same datetime. Consumers should handle both formats.
Versioning issue — the old API version returns Unix timestamps and the new version returns ISO strings.
Serialization bug — the ORM returns different formats depending on the database driver being used.

questions.explanation: Inconsistent field types violate the API contract — consumers can't reliably parse the field if its format changes between requests.

questions.question: An API endpoint averages 200ms response time. You add response time assertions to your tests with a 200ms threshold. Tests start failing intermittently. What's wrong with your approach?

options.text: 200ms is too strict. API response times vary naturally. Use a percentile-based threshold (e.g., p95 < 500ms over N requests) instead of asserting on individual request times.
The assertion is correct but should only run in production-like environments, not CI, where network conditions differ.
Add retry logic — if the first request is slow, retry and use the faster time for the assertion.
Run the performance assertion only once per test suite, not per test, to avoid network variability.

questions.explanation: Individual request times vary naturally — performance assertions should use statistical thresholds (percentiles over multiple requests) to avoid false failures.

questions.question: You're testing an API endpoint GET /api/users/42/orders. You authenticate as User 43 and request User 42's orders — the API returns them. What vulnerability is this?

options.text: Broken Object Level Authorization (BOLA/IDOR) — the API authenticates the user but doesn't verify they're authorized to access the specific resource. Any authenticated user can access any other user's data.
Broken authentication — the token for User 43 shouldn't work at all for User 42's endpoint.
Missing rate limiting — without rate limits, users can enumerate other users' data.
Insecure Direct Object Reference but it's low severity since the attacker would need to guess valid user IDs.

questions.explanation: BOLA/IDOR is the #1 API vulnerability (OWASP API Top 10) — the API must verify the authenticated user is authorized to access the specific requested resource.

questions.question: You send a PATCH request to update a user's email. The API returns 200 OK with the updated user object, but a subsequent GET returns the old email. What should you investigate?

options.text: Caching — the GET might be returning a cached response. The PATCH succeeded (200) but the GET is served from cache with stale data. Check response cache headers.
The PATCH endpoint is broken — it returns a fabricated response without actually persisting the change to the database.
Eventually consistent database — the write hasn't replicated to the read replica yet. Wait and retry the GET.
The API uses optimistic responses — it returns what the data will look like, but an async validation step may have rejected the email change.

questions.explanation: Stale cache on GET after a successful update is a common API bug — check Cache-Control headers and cache invalidation logic.

questions.question: Your API uses JSON:API spec. A consumer's tests break because a previously required field `phone` is now optional and sometimes absent from the response. The backend team says 'we didn't remove it, it's just nullable now.' Who's at fault?

options.text: The backend — changing a required field to optional is a breaking contract change even if the field still appears sometimes. It should have been versioned or communicated as a breaking change.
The consumer — their code should handle missing fields gracefully. Defensive parsing is a client responsibility.
Neither — JSON:API spec allows fields to be optional by default, so the consumer was relying on undocumented behavior.
The backend — but only because they should have returned `phone: null` instead of omitting the field entirely.

questions.explanation: Changing required fields to optional is a breaking change — API contracts must be versioned, and breaking changes require consumer notification and migration paths.

questions.question: An API handles 500 req/s normally. Under load testing at 2,000 req/s, response times degrade from 100ms to 15 seconds, and error rate jumps to 40%. After reducing load back to 500 req/s, response times stay at 5 seconds for 10 minutes. What's happening?

options.text: The service didn't recover because the load test created a cascading failure — connection pools exhausted, queues backed up, or thread pools saturated. The system lacks backpressure mechanisms to shed excess load and recover gracefully.
The load test corrupted the database indexes. Response times are slow because queries now do full table scans until indexes rebuild.
Rate limiting kicked in during the load test and hasn't expired yet. The API is throttling requests from the test client IP.
The server's CPU thermal throttling activated under sustained load and needs physical cooling time to return to normal clock speeds.

questions.explanation: Failure to recover after load reduction indicates missing backpressure — exhausted resources (connections, threads, queues) prevent recovery even after traffic drops.

questions.question: Your API accepts JSON input for a search feature: `{"query": "shoes"}`. You send `{"query": {"$gt": ""}}` and the API returns all products. What vulnerability did you discover?

options.text: NoSQL injection — the API passes user input directly into a MongoDB query without sanitization. The `$gt: ""` operator matches all documents, exposing the full dataset.
SQL injection variant — the JSON object is converted to a SQL LIKE clause that matches everything.
JSON schema bypass — the API accepts objects where it expects strings, but the query still executes safely against the database.
Mass assignment vulnerability — the extra JSON keys override internal query parameters.

questions.explanation: NoSQL injection via JSON operators — the API must validate input types and sanitize MongoDB query operators to prevent unauthorized data access.

questions.question: Your team decides to run a load test with 10,000 concurrent users. The app currently has 500 daily active users. Is this a good test plan?

options.text: Yes — you should always test at 20x your current traffic to prepare for viral growth.
No — load tests should be based on realistic traffic projections. Start with expected peak (e.g., 2-3x current) and gradually increase to find the breaking point.
Yes — it's better to over-test than under-test. You'll never regret being prepared for 10,000 users.
No — load testing an app with only 500 users is premature. Wait until you hit 5,000 users before investing in performance testing.

questions.explanation: Load tests should model realistic traffic patterns — testing at unrealistic volumes wastes resources and may optimize for scenarios that never occur.

questions.question: What's the difference between load testing and stress testing?

options.text: They're the same — both involve sending lots of requests to a server.
Load testing verifies performance under expected traffic. Stress testing pushes beyond capacity to find breaking points and observe how the system fails and recovers.
Load testing is automated, stress testing is manual. Testers manually interact with the system under stress.
Load testing uses production data, stress testing uses synthetic data.

questions.explanation: Load testing validates expected conditions; stress testing intentionally exceeds limits to discover failure modes and recovery behavior.

questions.question: A page takes 6 seconds to load. The backend API responds in 200ms. Where should you look next to find the bottleneck?

options.text: The backend is lying about response time — 200ms can't be accurate if the page takes 6 seconds.
The frontend — use browser DevTools to check network waterfall for large assets, render-blocking scripts, excessive DOM manipulation, or too many sequential API calls.
The database — slow queries are the most common performance bottleneck.
The CDN — 6 seconds indicates static assets are being served from a distant region.

questions.explanation: If the API is fast but the page is slow, the bottleneck is in the frontend — browser DevTools network/performance tabs reveal the actual cause.

questions.question: Your team sets a performance benchmark: 'API response time under 200ms.' After a deployment, response time is 195ms. A teammate says it passes. What's the concern?

options.text: 195ms is too close to the 200ms threshold — any variance could push it over. The trend matters more than a single measurement. If it was 150ms last week and 195ms now, performance is degrading.
No concern — 195ms is under 200ms. The benchmark is met.
The benchmark should be 100ms, not 200ms. Modern APIs should be under 100ms.
195ms is only valid if measured under production load. Test environment measurements are always meaningless.

questions.explanation: Performance trending is as important as absolute thresholds — a measurement near the limit that's been increasing over time signals degradation before it becomes a failure.

questions.question: You run a load test ramping from 0 to 1,000 users over 10 minutes. Response times are stable at 100ms until 600 users, then spike to 2 seconds. What does this inflection point tell you?

options.text: A resource hit its limit at 600 concurrent users — likely a connection pool, thread pool, or database connection limit. The system can't scale beyond this without addressing the bottleneck.
The load test tool is the bottleneck. At 600 users, the test client can't generate requests fast enough.
Network bandwidth saturated at 600 users. Larger server NICs needed.
600 users is the server's designed capacity. Response times above this are expected and acceptable.

questions.explanation: A sharp inflection point indicates a resource ceiling — identifying which resource (connections, threads, CPU, memory) saturates at that level guides optimization.

questions.question: During a stress test, your API starts returning 503 Service Unavailable at 3,000 req/s. After reducing to 500 req/s, it recovers in 30 seconds. Another API in the same scenario takes 15 minutes to recover. Which system is better designed?

options.text: The 30-second recovery system — it has proper backpressure mechanisms (circuit breakers, queue limits, connection pool timeouts) that allow quick recovery when load drops.
The 15-minute system — it handles more load before failing because it queues requests instead of rejecting them.
Neither is well-designed — a properly built system should never return 503 regardless of load.
Both are equivalent — recovery time is determined by cloud provider auto-scaling speed, not system design.

questions.explanation: Fast recovery under reduced load indicates good resilience design — circuit breakers and load shedding prevent cascading failures and enable quick recovery.

questions.question: A Node.js API has 99th percentile latency of 3 seconds. CPU usage is only 30%. Memory is stable. What's the most likely bottleneck?

options.text: Node.js event loop blocking — a synchronous operation (file I/O, heavy computation, JSON parsing of large payloads) is blocking the single thread, causing requests to queue.
Low CPU usage means the server is underloaded. Add more traffic to fully utilize the CPU.
The 99th percentile is always high in Node.js due to garbage collection pauses. This is normal.
Network latency between the load test client and the server adds 3 seconds of round-trip time.

questions.explanation: Low CPU + high latency in Node.js typically means event loop blocking — synchronous operations prevent the single thread from processing other requests.

questions.question: Your performance benchmarks show the API averages 100ms in staging but 300ms in production. Infrastructure is identical. What's the most likely explanation?

options.text: Production has real data — larger database tables, more complex queries, fuller caches. Staging with minimal test data doesn't reflect real-world query patterns and data volumes.
Production network latency is always 3x staging because of security layers (WAF, DDoS protection).
Staging runs in debug mode which is paradoxically faster due to disabled logging.
Production deployments are always slower due to container orchestration overhead.

questions.explanation: Data volume is the most common staging-vs-production discrepancy — benchmarks should use production-representative data volumes and query patterns.

questions.question: You run a soak test (sustained load) for 8 hours. Performance is fine for 6 hours, then response times gradually increase. CPU and request rate stay constant. What's happening?

options.text: Memory leak — objects accumulate over time, increasing garbage collection pressure and eventually degrading performance. The gradual onset after 6 hours is a classic memory leak signature.
Database connection pool leak — connections are borrowed but not returned, eventually exhausting the pool.
Log files filling the disk — as available disk space decreases, write operations slow down.
TLS session cache growing unbounded — each new connection adds to the cache, increasing lookup time.

questions.explanation: Gradual performance degradation over hours with constant load is the signature of a memory leak — resources accumulate until GC pressure or OOM degrades performance.

questions.question: Your microservice architecture handles 10,000 req/s. Service A calls Service B which calls Service C. Under stress, Service C slows from 50ms to 5 seconds. What happens to Services A and B if there are no timeouts configured?

options.text: Cascading failure — B's threads block waiting for C, exhausting B's thread pool. A then blocks waiting for B. All three services become unresponsive even though only C has the actual problem.
Only Service C is affected. Services A and B have independent thread pools and will continue serving other requests normally.
Load balancers automatically reroute traffic away from slow services, protecting A and B.
Kubernetes will restart Service C within 30 seconds, resolving the issue before A and B are affected.

questions.explanation: Without timeouts and circuit breakers, slow dependencies cascade upstream — blocked threads propagate through the call chain, taking down the entire system.

questions.question: A database query takes 50ms with 10,000 rows but 12 seconds with 1,000,000 rows. The query uses WHERE on an indexed column. What profiling step reveals the root cause?

options.text: Run EXPLAIN ANALYZE on the query. The database may choose a sequential scan over the index at high row counts if the selectivity is too low — the optimizer decides a full scan is cheaper than index lookups when the query matches too many rows.
Check network bandwidth — 1M rows generates too much data transfer between database and application.
Rebuild the index — indexes degrade over time and need periodic rebuilding at large data volumes.
Switch to a NoSQL database — relational databases can't handle million-row tables efficiently.

questions.explanation: EXPLAIN ANALYZE reveals the execution plan — the optimizer may abandon the index for a sequential scan when query selectivity is low on large tables.

questions.question: You benchmark two implementations: Version A averages 10ms, Version B averages 12ms. The team picks A. You argue this is premature. Why?

options.text: Averages hide distribution. Check p50, p95, p99. Version B might have p99=15ms while Version A has p99=500ms (occasional long GC pauses). Also check: sample size, warm-up period, and confidence intervals.
2ms difference is within measurement error. Run the benchmark for 24 hours to get a statistically significant result.
Benchmarks should use production data. Synthetic benchmarks are meaningless for decision-making.
Average response time doesn't account for throughput. Version B might handle 2x the concurrent requests.

questions.explanation: Averages mask tail latency — percentile distributions, sample size, warm-up, and statistical significance must all be considered before drawing benchmark conclusions.

questions.question: Your mobile app works on Android 14 but crashes on Android 11. Both use the same APK. What's the most likely cause?

options.text: The app uses an API introduced in Android 12+ without checking the OS version first. Calling unavailable APIs on older versions causes runtime crashes.
Android 11 doesn't support the APK format used by modern build tools.
The older device has less RAM, causing out-of-memory crashes unrelated to OS version.
Google Play Services on Android 11 is outdated, blocking app startup.

questions.explanation: Using APIs not available on older OS versions without version checks is the most common cross-version crash — always check Build.VERSION.SDK_INT before using newer APIs.

questions.question: A user reports that swiping left to delete an item in a list sometimes accidentally opens the item detail instead. What should you test?

options.text: The gesture recognition threshold — how far horizontally must the user swipe before it registers as 'swipe left' versus a tap? The threshold may be too high, causing short swipes to register as taps.
Device screen sensitivity — some screens register touches less accurately than others.
The animation speed — if the delete animation is too fast, users can't see the confirmation and accidentally tap.
The item detail view — it shouldn't open on tap, only on a dedicated 'open' button.

questions.explanation: Gesture conflicts between swipe and tap arise from recognition thresholds — test the minimum swipe distance, angle tolerance, and velocity needed to distinguish gestures.

questions.question: Your mobile app works perfectly on WiFi but shows blank screens on cellular data. API calls work in Postman over cellular. What should you check?

options.text: The app's network security config may block cleartext (HTTP) traffic, and the cellular provider's proxy downgrades HTTPS to HTTP, breaking the connection.
The app downloads large payloads that work on WiFi but timeout on slower cellular connections. Check response sizes and implement proper loading states.
Cellular data has a different IP range blocked by the API server's firewall.
The device's cellular antenna is damaged, causing intermittent connectivity.

questions.explanation: Blank screens on cellular often indicate timeout issues with large payloads on slower connections — test with throttled networks and implement progressive loading.

questions.question: Your app looks perfect on the test device (Pixel 7) but text is cut off on a Samsung Galaxy A14. What's the most likely issue?

options.text: Samsung devices use a proprietary rendering engine that handles text differently.
Different screen sizes, densities, and default font sizes across devices. The layout doesn't adapt responsively — fixed-size elements clip text on smaller or differently-scaled screens.
The Galaxy A14 has a software bug in its text rendering library.
Samsung's One UI applies additional padding to all text elements, reducing available space.

questions.explanation: Device fragmentation means varying screen sizes and densities — layouts must be responsive, and text should handle different font scales gracefully.

questions.question: Your iOS app passes all tests, but App Store review rejects it saying 'app doesn't work with iPadOS multitasking.' What did your testing miss?

options.text: Apple requires iPad apps to support Split View and Slide Over multitasking. Testing only on iPhone missed iPad-specific layout requirements where the app must function at various split-screen widths.
App Store reviewers use older iPads with iPadOS 15 — your app only supports iPadOS 17+.
The app binary wasn't compiled with iPad support enabled in Xcode.
Apple requires a separate iPad version. Universal apps aren't accepted on the App Store.

questions.explanation: Apple requires iPad apps to support multitasking (Split View, Slide Over) — testing must include various split-screen widths, not just full-screen iPhone layouts.

questions.question: Your app uses pinch-to-zoom on a map. A tester reports that double-tapping to zoom also triggers the 'place marker' action bound to single-tap. How should this be handled?

options.text: Implement a tap delay (200-300ms) to distinguish single-tap from double-tap. If a second tap arrives within the window, treat it as double-tap zoom; otherwise, fire the marker placement.
Remove the double-tap zoom feature — pinch-to-zoom is sufficient and eliminates the conflict.
Require a long-press instead of single-tap for marker placement, avoiding any conflict with double-tap.
Undo the marker placement automatically after detecting the double-tap zoom that follows.

questions.explanation: Single-tap vs. double-tap disambiguation requires a brief delay to determine user intent — this is a standard gesture recognition pattern in mobile development.

questions.question: A user is filling out a long form in your app. They enter an elevator, lose connectivity, and tap Submit when they exit. The app shows 'Network Error' and the form data is gone. What should you test for?

options.text: The app should queue the submission and retry when connectivity returns, or at minimum preserve form data locally so the user doesn't have to re-enter it after a network failure.
The app should prevent form submission when offline by disabling the Submit button.
This is expected behavior — users should ensure stable connectivity before submitting important data.
The app should detect connectivity changes and warn users before they enter low-signal areas.

questions.explanation: Mobile apps must handle connectivity transitions gracefully — persist form data locally and queue or retry failed submissions when connectivity resumes.

questions.question: Your app runs smoothly on flagship phones but stutters visibly on mid-range devices with 3GB RAM. What performance testing approach helps?

options.text: Test exclusively on the lowest-end supported device — if it works there, it works everywhere.
Profile the app on mid-range devices to identify memory-heavy operations, excessive background tasks, or unoptimized rendering. Set performance budgets based on target device tiers.
Drop support for devices under 6GB RAM — mid-range devices represent too small a market share to optimize for.
Enable Android's developer option to simulate low memory on flagship devices instead of testing on real mid-range hardware.

questions.explanation: Profiling on representative mid-range devices reveals actual bottlenecks — set performance budgets per device tier to ensure acceptable experience across target hardware.

questions.question: After updating your app's target SDK to Android 13, users report that push notifications stopped working. The app code hasn't changed. What happened?

options.text: Android 13 requires apps to explicitly request the POST_NOTIFICATIONS runtime permission. Targeting the new SDK enforces this — without the permission request, notifications are silently blocked.
Google changed the push notification API in Android 13, requiring a code migration to the new FCM v2 SDK.
Android 13 blocks notifications from apps that haven't been opened in 30 days as a battery optimization.
The notification channel created for older SDKs is incompatible with Android 13's notification categories.

questions.explanation: Android 13 introduced POST_NOTIFICATIONS as a runtime permission — targeting SDK 33+ requires explicitly requesting it, or notifications are silently suppressed.

questions.question: Your app has a carousel that supports horizontal swiping. It's embedded inside a vertically scrollable page. Users report the page scrolls vertically when they try to swipe the carousel horizontally. What's the root cause?

options.text: Nested scroll conflict — the parent scroll view intercepts touch events before the carousel can process them. The gesture angle detection doesn't properly determine if the user intends horizontal or vertical movement at the start of the touch.
The carousel component doesn't support horizontal scrolling natively and falls back to the parent's vertical scroll.
Mobile browsers don't support nested scrollable containers — this is a platform limitation.
The carousel's z-index is lower than the parent scroll view, so touch events never reach it.

questions.explanation: Nested scroll conflicts require proper touch event interception — the inner container must claim horizontal gestures while passing vertical ones to the parent.

questions.question: Your app makes API calls that work on WiFi and 4G but fail on some public WiFi networks. The error is a TLS handshake failure. What's happening?

options.text: Public WiFi captive portals intercept HTTPS traffic with their own certificate, causing TLS verification to fail. The app should detect captive portals and prompt users to authenticate with the WiFi network before making API calls.
Public WiFi networks use older encryption that's incompatible with TLS 1.3.
The WiFi network's DNS blocks the API domain, and the TLS error is misleading.
Public WiFi bandwidth is too low for TLS handshake packets, causing timeouts interpreted as failures.

questions.explanation: Captive portals on public WiFi intercept HTTPS with substitute certificates, causing TLS failures — apps should detect and handle captive portal states.

questions.question: Your app stores user data in local storage. A user with a 32GB phone (only 1GB free) reports data loss after the phone showed a 'storage full' warning. What happened and what should you test?

options.text: The OS may clear app cache/data when storage is critically low. Test behavior when writes fail due to full storage — the app should catch write failures, warn users, and never silently lose data.
32GB phones can't handle the app's data requirements. Set minimum storage as 64GB in the app store listing.
The user accidentally cleared app data from Settings while trying to free space.
Local storage has a fixed quota per app. When the device is full, the quota shrinks and oldest data is evicted.

questions.explanation: Low-storage conditions cause write failures and potential OS-initiated cache clearing — apps must handle storage errors gracefully and protect critical user data.

questions.question: You enter `<script>alert('test')</script>` into a comment field and see an alert box pop up. What vulnerability did you find?

options.text: SQL injection — the script tag was executed as a database command.
Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) — the application rendered user input as executable HTML/JavaScript without sanitization, allowing arbitrary code execution in other users' browsers.
CSRF — the form submitted the comment to a malicious endpoint.
Remote Code Execution — the script ran on the server, not the browser.

questions.explanation: XSS occurs when user input is rendered as executable code in the browser — the application must sanitize or encode all user-provided content before rendering.

questions.question: Before starting a penetration test on your company's production system, what must happen first?

options.text: Nothing — pen testing your own company's systems is always allowed.
Get written authorization defining scope, timing, and methods. Unscoped pen testing can cause outages, legal issues, and trigger security alarms even on your own systems.
Inform the CEO — executive approval is sufficient authorization for any security testing.
Set up a staging environment — pen testing should never be done on production systems under any circumstances.

questions.explanation: Written authorization with defined scope is mandatory — unscoped testing can cause outages, trigger incident response, and create legal liability.

questions.question: A developer says XSS is prevented because they block `<script>` tags in user input. Is the application safe?

options.text: Yes — blocking script tags prevents all JavaScript execution.
No — XSS can be achieved through many vectors beyond script tags: event handlers (onerror, onload), javascript: URLs, SVG elements, CSS expressions, and encoded payloads that bypass simple filters.
Yes — as long as they also block <iframe> and <object> tags, all XSS vectors are covered.
No — but only because the filter can be bypassed with case variations like <SCRIPT> or <ScRiPt>.

questions.explanation: Blocking script tags alone is insufficient — XSS has dozens of vectors including event handlers, protocol handlers, and encoding tricks. Use output encoding, not input blocking.

questions.question: You notice that after logging out of a web application, pressing the browser's back button shows the previous authenticated page with user data visible. Is this a security issue?

options.text: No — the browser is showing a cached page. No new data is fetched from the server.
Yes — authenticated pages should set proper cache-control headers (no-store, no-cache) to prevent browsers from displaying sensitive content after logout, especially on shared devices.
No — this is browser behavior that applications can't control.
Yes — but only if the page contains financial data. For non-sensitive pages, caching after logout is acceptable.

questions.explanation: Sensitive pages must use cache-control headers to prevent post-logout display — without them, anyone with physical access can view cached authenticated content.

questions.question: You test a login form by entering `admin' OR '1'='1` as the username and any password. You're logged in as admin. What happened?

options.text: SQL injection — the application concatenates user input directly into SQL queries. The injected OR clause makes the WHERE condition always true, bypassing password verification.
The admin account has no password set, so any password works. The special characters in the username are coincidental.
The application has a backdoor admin login that accepts any credentials containing the word 'admin'.
LDAP injection — the query bypassed the directory service authentication.

questions.explanation: Classic SQL injection — input concatenated into queries changes query logic. Fix: use parameterized queries/prepared statements, never string concatenation.

questions.question: During a pen test, you discover an open admin panel at /admin with default credentials admin/admin. The client says 'it's internal only, behind a VPN.' Why is this still a risk?

options.text: VPN doesn't prevent insider threats, compromised VPN credentials, or lateral movement from other breached internal systems. Defense in depth requires strong auth even behind VPNs.
VPNs can be bypassed by DNS rebinding attacks that make external requests appear internal.
Default credentials are automatically detected by vulnerability scanners that run inside the VPN.
Compliance regulations (SOC2, HIPAA) explicitly prohibit default credentials regardless of network location.

questions.explanation: VPN is one layer, not sufficient alone — insider threats, VPN credential leaks, and lateral movement from other breaches all bypass network-level protection.

questions.question: A web app sanitizes user input on the server before storing it. A tester discovers that typing `javascript:alert(1)` in a profile URL field doesn't trigger XSS on the profile page, but the stored value is still `javascript:alert(1)`. Is the app safe?

options.text: Yes — the XSS didn't trigger, so the sanitization is working correctly.

No — the stored value is unsanitized. If any other page renders this URL as an href attribute, clicking it executes JavaScript. The profile page may escape it, but other consumers of this data may not.
Yes — javascript: protocol URLs are blocked by all modern browsers.
No — but only because the URL field should validate that input is a valid HTTP/HTTPS URL, not because of XSS risk.

questions.explanation: Stored unsanitized data is a ticking bomb — even if one page escapes it, other pages or future features consuming the same data may render it unsafely.

questions.question: An application's password reset sends a link with a token: `/reset?token=12345`. The token is a sequential integer. What's the security problem?

options.text: Sequential tokens are guessable — an attacker can iterate through token values (12344, 12346, etc.) to hijack other users' password resets. Tokens must be cryptographically random and time-limited.
The token is too short. Using a longer sequential number (e.g., 12-digit) would be sufficiently secure.
The token appears in the URL, which is logged by web servers. Token should be in the request body instead.
HTTP is insecure for password reset links. The issue is the protocol, not the token format.

questions.explanation: Predictable tokens allow account takeover — password reset tokens must be cryptographically random, single-use, and time-limited to prevent enumeration attacks.

questions.question: An API endpoint accepts a file upload. You upload a PHP file disguised as a JPEG (renamed to photo.jpg). The server stores it in a publicly accessible directory. What can you test next?

options.text: Try accessing the uploaded file directly via URL. If the server executes PHP files in the upload directory, you may achieve Remote Code Execution — the server must validate file content (not just extension) and store uploads outside the web root.
The test is complete — the server accepted the file without error, which is the expected behavior for a file upload endpoint.
Check if the file corrupted any existing files in the upload directory.
Verify the file was stored with correct JPEG metadata despite being PHP content.

questions.explanation: Unrestricted file upload with public storage is an RCE vector — validate file content (magic bytes, not just extension) and store outside web root or use object storage.

questions.question: During a pen test, you find an SSRF vulnerability — the app fetches URLs provided in user input. You send `http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/` and receive AWS instance metadata. What's the impact?

options.text: The metadata endpoint exposes IAM role credentials, which can be used to access other AWS services (S3, RDS, etc.) with the instance's permissions — potentially full cloud infrastructure compromise.
Limited impact — instance metadata only contains the server's hostname and IP address.
The metadata is read-only. An attacker can see configuration but can't modify anything.
AWS blocks metadata access from applications by default — this only works in misconfigured instances.

questions.explanation: SSRF to cloud metadata is critical — IAM role credentials from 169.254.169.254 enable lateral movement across the cloud infrastructure. Use IMDSv2 to mitigate.

questions.question: A React application uses `dangerouslySetInnerHTML` to render user-generated content from a WYSIWYG editor. The developer sanitizes input with DOMPurify before storage. A QA engineer says this is still risky. Why?

options.text: DOMPurify may have bypass vulnerabilities discovered after deployment. If sanitization only happens at write time, stored content remains unsanitized against future bypass discoveries. Sanitize at render time too for defense in depth.
DOMPurify doesn't work with React's virtual DOM, making it ineffective.
WYSIWYG editors always produce valid HTML that doesn't need sanitization — DOMPurify is unnecessary overhead.
dangerouslySetInnerHTML automatically sanitizes content in React 18+, making DOMPurify redundant.

questions.explanation: Sanitize at render time, not just storage time — new bypass techniques are discovered regularly, and re-sanitization protects against future DOMPurify vulnerabilities.

questions.question: An application uses JWT tokens with a 24-hour expiry. There's no token revocation mechanism. A user's account is compromised and their password is changed. What's the security gap?

options.text: The attacker's existing JWT remains valid for up to 24 hours after the password change because JWTs are self-contained — without a revocation list or short-lived tokens with refresh rotation, the old token grants continued access.
Changing the password automatically invalidates all JWTs because the signing key changes.
24-hour expiry is short enough that the risk window is acceptable for most applications.
The JWT will fail on next refresh because the refresh token checks the current password hash.

questions.explanation: JWTs without revocation allow compromised tokens to remain valid until expiry — implement token revocation lists, short expiry + refresh rotation, or session binding.

questions.question: You need test data for a user registration form. A junior tester creates 50 users all named 'Test User 1', 'Test User 2', etc. with sequential emails. What's the problem?

options.text: Nothing — sequential data is efficient and easy to manage in test environments.

Uniform test data misses real-world edge cases. Use varied data: special characters in names, international formats, long/short values, and realistic distributions to uncover bugs that homogeneous data hides.
50 users isn't enough. Generate at least 10,000 users for meaningful test coverage.
Sequential IDs create a security risk if the test database is accidentally exposed.

questions.explanation: Homogeneous test data misses edge cases — real users have accented characters, long names, hyphens, apostrophes, and diverse email formats that reveal bugs.

questions.question: Test A creates an order, Test B checks order count. When run alone, both pass. When run together, Test B fails because it counts Test A's order. What's the fix?

options.text: Run tests in a fixed order so Test B always runs before Test A.
Each test should set up its own data and clean up afterward (or use transactions that roll back). Tests must be independent and not rely on or be affected by other tests' data.
Use a shared counter that tracks how many orders each test creates and adjusts assertions accordingly.
Run each test in a separate test environment to isolate data completely.

questions.explanation: Test isolation is fundamental — each test should create its own data and clean up, ensuring tests pass regardless of execution order or other tests' side effects.

questions.question: A developer copies the production database to the staging environment for realistic testing. The database contains customer names, emails, and credit card numbers. What's the immediate concern?

options.text: Performance — production databases are too large for staging environments.
Privacy violation — real customer PII and payment data in non-production environments violates data protection regulations (GDPR, PCI-DSS). Data must be anonymized or masked before copying.
Data freshness — production data becomes stale quickly and tests against old data are unreliable.
License cost — staging environments have separate database licenses that don't cover production data.

questions.explanation: Real PII in non-production environments violates privacy regulations — always anonymize, mask, or use synthetic data for testing.

questions.question: Tests pass in the dev environment but fail in staging with 'record not found' errors. The application code is identical. What's the most likely cause?

options.text: Staging has network restrictions blocking database connections.
Tests depend on specific data records that exist in the dev database but not in staging. Test data setup is environment-specific instead of creating required data as part of test execution.
Staging uses a different database engine (PostgreSQL vs MySQL) that handles queries differently.
The staging database hasn't been migrated to the latest schema version.

questions.explanation: Environment-dependent test data causes cross-environment failures — tests should create their own required data rather than depending on pre-existing records.

questions.question: You need to test a search feature with 1 million product records. Creating them manually is impractical. What's the best approach?

options.text: Use a data generation library (Faker, Factory Bot) with realistic distributions — varied product names, prices across ranges, categories, and edge cases. Seed the generator for reproducibility.
Copy a subset of production data (10,000 records) and duplicate it 100 times to reach 1 million.
Generate 1 million identical records with different IDs — the search feature only cares about volume, not variety.
Use an AI to generate realistic product descriptions and prices for each of the 1 million records.

questions.explanation: Data generation libraries produce varied, realistic test data at scale — seeded generators ensure reproducibility while maintaining the diversity needed to find bugs.

questions.question: Your test suite takes 30 minutes. Investigation shows 60% of the time is spent on test data setup and teardown (creating/deleting database records). How do you speed this up?

options.text: Skip teardown — let data accumulate and reset the database nightly.
Use database transactions for test isolation — start a transaction before each test and roll back after. No data actually persists, so there's nothing to clean up.
Create all test data once in a shared setup and never delete it. Tests should be written to work with shared data.
Move test data to in-memory SQLite instead of the real database. Tests run faster against in-memory storage.

questions.explanation: Transaction-based isolation wraps each test in a transaction that rolls back — instant cleanup, no residual data, and much faster than explicit insert/delete cycles.

questions.question: Your team needs to debug a production issue that only occurs with a specific customer's data. The customer's record includes protected health information (PHI). What's the correct approach?

options.text: Copy the customer's record to a local dev environment for debugging — speed of resolution justifies temporary access.
Create a synthetic record that replicates the data characteristics causing the issue (field lengths, formats, special characters) without using actual PHI. Debug against the synthetic reproduction.
Give all developers temporary production database access with an NDA. After debugging, revoke access.
Ask the customer's permission to use their data for debugging purposes.

questions.explanation: Reproduce the issue with synthetic data that matches the problematic data's characteristics — PHI should never leave production for debugging purposes.

questions.question: Your staging environment has 1,000 test records. Production has 10 million records. A query that takes 50ms in staging takes 30 seconds in production. What does this reveal about your test environment?

options.text: The test environment's data volume is too small to catch performance issues. Test environments should contain production-representative data volumes or at least enough data to exercise the same query execution plans.
The production database needs optimization — indexes, query tuning, or hardware upgrades.
This is expected — production will always be slower due to concurrent users, and test environments can't replicate that.
The staging database uses different hardware. Match the hardware specs to production.

questions.explanation: Small test datasets don't trigger the same query plans as production volumes — database optimizers choose different strategies at different data sizes.

questions.question: You generate 10,000 test users with a Faker library. All generated phone numbers are US format. Your app supports international users. What's missing?

options.text: Faker defaults to one locale. Configure multiple locales to generate international phone formats, addresses, and names — test data should represent your actual user demographics including non-ASCII characters and varying format lengths.
Phone number format doesn't matter — the app normalizes all numbers to a standard format before storing.
Generate US numbers and manually prepend international country codes — this is sufficient for format testing.
International number support should be tested separately with a dedicated international test suite.

questions.explanation: Test data must reflect real user diversity — configure data generators with multiple locales to produce international formats, characters, and conventions.

questions.question: Your team uses a shared test database. Multiple testers run suites simultaneously. Tests intermittently fail due to data conflicts. What's the root cause and best solution?

options.text: Shared mutable state — concurrent test runs modify the same data. Use unique namespacing per test run (unique prefixes/suffixes on created records) or give each test run an isolated database schema or container.
Database connection pooling limits — too many concurrent connections cause query timeouts.
Lock the database per test run — only one tester can execute at a time using a distributed lock.
Increase database instance size to handle concurrent load from multiple test runs.

questions.explanation: Shared test databases with concurrent access cause data conflicts — isolate via namespaced data, per-run schemas, or containerized database instances.

questions.question: Your data masking script replaces real emails with `test1@example.com`, `test2@example.com`, etc. A QA engineer says this masking is insufficient for testing. Why?

options.text: Generic masked emails lose the diversity of real data — they won't test handling of long emails, subdomains, plus-addressing (user+tag@), international TLDs, or case sensitivity. Masking should preserve data characteristics while removing PII.
example.com is a reserved domain that email validation libraries reject.
