Sequential test emails make it easy to re-identify original users by matching order to the original dataset.
The masking script doesn't handle emails stored in JSON blobs or log files, only database columns.

questions.explanation: Effective masking preserves data characteristics (length, format variety, special characters) while removing PII — uniform replacement loses the testing value of diverse data.

questions.question: Your CI tests use an in-memory H2 database while production uses PostgreSQL. A tester finds a bug where a case-insensitive LIKE query works in CI but fails in production. What's the lesson?

options.text: Database engines have behavioral differences — H2's LIKE is case-insensitive by default, PostgreSQL's is case-sensitive. Tests against a different database engine give false confidence. Use the same database engine in CI as production.
The bug is in the application code, not the database. The query should use a database-agnostic case-insensitive function.
H2 is only for unit tests. Integration tests should always use PostgreSQL, but unit tests are fine with H2.
Add a compatibility layer that normalizes query behavior across database engines.

questions.explanation: Different database engines have different SQL behaviors — testing against a substitute database hides real bugs. Use the same engine in all environments.

questions.question: A tester files a bug report: 'The page is broken.' A developer marks it 'needs more info.' What should the bug report include instead?

options.text: Steps to reproduce, expected behavior, actual behavior, environment details (browser/OS/version), and screenshots or logs. Enough for a developer to reproduce and fix without asking follow-up questions.
Just a screenshot of the broken page — a picture is worth a thousand words.
The URL of the broken page and the tester's username so the developer can log in as them.
A suggested code fix — testers should diagnose root causes before filing bugs.

questions.explanation: Complete bug reports include reproduction steps, expected vs. actual behavior, environment details, and evidence — enabling developers to reproduce without back-and-forth.

questions.question: The bug backlog has 200 open issues. The team can fix 20 per sprint. How should you prioritize?

options.text: Fix bugs in the order they were reported — first-in, first-out is fair.
Triage by severity and user impact. Critical bugs affecting many users first, then high-severity, then medium. Cosmetic issues and edge cases go to the backlog.
Fix the easiest bugs first to reduce the backlog count quickly.
Let developers choose which bugs to fix based on their expertise and interest.

questions.explanation: Triage by impact ensures the most damaging bugs are fixed first — severity and user impact should drive prioritization, not age or difficulty.

questions.question: A user reports a crash but you can't reproduce it on your machine. What should you check first?

options.text: Close the bug as 'cannot reproduce' — if it can't be reproduced, it can't be fixed.
Compare environments: user's OS, browser version, screen resolution, account type, data state, and network conditions. Reproduction failures usually mean different conditions, not a phantom bug.
Ask the user to send a screen recording. If they can't, the bug probably doesn't exist.
Escalate to the development team immediately — they have better debugging tools to reproduce it.

questions.explanation: Reproduction failures indicate environmental differences — systematically match the user's conditions (browser, OS, data, account) to replicate the issue.

questions.question: Management asks for the 'defect leakage rate' metric. What does this measure?

options.text: The percentage of bugs that escape testing and reach production. A lower rate means testing is catching more defects before release.
The rate at which bug reports are submitted — higher means the team is finding more bugs.
How quickly bugs leak from one sprint's backlog to the next when not fixed on time.
The percentage of test cases that fail during regression testing.

questions.explanation: Defect leakage rate measures bugs found in production vs. total bugs — it indicates testing effectiveness at catching defects before release.

questions.question: A tester reports: 'Login fails with error 500.' The developer can't reproduce it. Later, the tester mentions it only happens when the password contains a backtick (`). What bug reporting skill was missing?

options.text: Minimal reproduction case — the tester should have isolated the exact input that triggers the bug. Narrowing 'login fails' to 'login fails when password contains backtick' is critical for efficient debugging.
The tester should have inspected the server logs and included the stack trace in the report.
The tester should have tested with multiple passwords before filing — one failure isn't enough to file a bug.
The developer should have tested all special characters. This is a developer responsibility, not a reporting issue.

questions.explanation: Isolating the minimal reproduction case (the specific input, condition, or step that triggers the bug) dramatically reduces debugging time.

questions.question: During triage, you encounter two bugs: Bug A crashes the app for 0.1% of users on an obscure device, Bug B causes a cosmetic alignment issue visible to all users on the homepage. Which gets higher priority?

options.text: Bug A — crashes are always higher priority than cosmetic issues, regardless of user count.
Bug B — it affects 100% of users on the most visible page. Visual defects on the homepage impact brand perception and user trust more than a crash affecting 0.1%.

It depends on business context. For a banking app, the crash is critical despite low occurrence. For a content site, the homepage cosmetic issue matters more. Triage considers both severity and business impact.
Both get the same priority — bugs should be prioritized by age, not severity or reach.

questions.explanation: Effective triage considers severity, reach, and business context — the 'right' priority depends on the application domain and user expectations.

questions.question: A bug occurs only in production, never in any test environment. Error logs show a NullPointerException in the user profile service. What reproduction strategy should you try?

options.text: Analyze production logs for the exact request parameters, user account state, and timing. Replicate those conditions in a test environment — the bug likely depends on specific data states (null fields in legacy accounts, migration artifacts) that test data doesn't include.
Add more logging to the production code and wait for the bug to happen again.
Debug directly in production using a remote debugger attached to the running service.
This is a Heisenbug — it disappears when observed. Mark it as 'cannot reproduce' and close if it doesn't recur in 30 days.

questions.explanation: Production-only bugs usually depend on specific data states — analyze logs for exact conditions and replicate those conditions (account state, data patterns) in test environments.

questions.question: Your team's bug fix rate is 30 bugs/sprint, but the discovery rate is 40 bugs/sprint. The backlog grows by 10 every sprint. What metric should you present to management?

options.text: Bug escape rate — focus on preventing bugs from being created in the first place.

Bug debt trend — show the growing backlog trajectory and project when it becomes unmanageable. Use this to justify investment in either more fix capacity or root cause analysis to reduce the inflow rate.
The 30 bugs/sprint fix rate — focus on the positive: the team is highly productive at fixing bugs.
Individual developer bug counts — identify which developers create the most bugs for targeted training.

questions.explanation: A growing bug backlog is unsustainable — trending the debt growth and projecting its impact drives decisions about capacity investment or process improvements.

questions.question: You find a bug where the 'Add to Cart' button doesn't work on Safari 17 when the user is logged in. Your bug report should include which environment details?

options.text: Just 'Safari' — the browser name is sufficient for the developer to investigate.
Browser and version (Safari 17.x), OS (macOS Sonoma 14.x), device, login state (authenticated), and whether it reproduces in private browsing mode — plus confirmation it works in other browsers.
The user agent string — it contains all the environment information a developer needs.
Whatever your bug template asks for. If the template only has a browser field, just fill in Safari.

questions.explanation: Precise environment details narrow the investigation — browser version, OS, auth state, and browser mode all affect reproducibility and root cause analysis.

questions.question: After a release, 50 new bugs are filed in 48 hours. The team suspects many are duplicates or symptoms of the same root cause. How do you triage efficiently?

options.text: Cluster bugs by affected area, error type, and timing. Look for a common root cause — 50 bugs might stem from 3-5 underlying issues. Fix root causes first, then verify which reported bugs resolve automatically.
Assign each bug to a different developer for fastest resolution. 50 developers fixing 50 bugs simultaneously is optimal.
Rollback the release immediately — 50 bugs in 48 hours indicates a fundamentally broken release.
Close all 50 as duplicates and create one 'post-release issues' mega-bug for investigation.

questions.explanation: Clustering by symptoms reveals root causes — many reported bugs are often manifestations of a few underlying issues. Fix root causes to resolve multiple reports at once.

questions.question: A race condition bug occurs approximately once every 500 test runs. You need to reproduce it reliably for debugging. What's the most effective approach?

options.text: Introduce deliberate delays (Thread.sleep or artificial latency) at suspected race points to widen the timing window, or use a tool like ThreadSanitizer/Go race detector that instruments code to detect data races deterministically.
Run the test 500 times in a loop until it fails, then capture the failing state.
Increase the test timeout — race conditions occur when the system is too fast, and slower execution eliminates them.
Race conditions are inherently unreproducible. Add retry logic and accept the occasional failure.

questions.explanation: Deliberate timing manipulation or race detection tools make non-deterministic race conditions reproducible — running tests in a loop is inefficient and uninformative.

questions.question: Your defect density metric shows Module A has 2 bugs/KLOC and Module B has 15 bugs/KLOC. Management wants to rewrite Module B. Why might this be the wrong conclusion?

options.text: Defect density without context is misleading. Module B might be more complex, more thoroughly tested, more recently refactored (surfacing latent bugs), or in an area with higher user traffic (more reporting). Low defect density can mean undertesting, not better code.
Defect density should be measured per feature, not per KLOC. Module B might have fewer features in more code.
The metric is calculated wrong — it should use function points, not lines of code.
Module B's higher density is purely because it's newer. All new code has more bugs than mature code.

questions.explanation: Defect density without context misleads — high density can mean better testing, more usage, or higher complexity, not necessarily worse code quality.

questions.question: A developer pushes code and the CI pipeline runs tests, but the tests use the previous version of the code. What's the most likely configuration issue?

options.text: The CI pipeline isn't checking out the latest commit. It's using a cached workspace from a previous run or checking out the wrong branch/ref.
Git hasn't synced yet — there's a delay between push and CI availability.
The test files are cached by the CI runner and use a stale copy of the test suite.
The developer forgot to commit the test files. The code changes were pushed but test updates are still local.

questions.explanation: CI pipelines must check out the exact commit that triggered the build — misconfigured checkout steps or workspace caching can run tests against stale code.

questions.question: Your CI pipeline runs unit tests. A developer pushes code that breaks an integration test, but the pipeline passes and the code is merged. What's missing?

options.text: The pipeline only runs unit tests. Integration tests should also be a required gate before merge — a pipeline that only catches unit-level failures misses interaction bugs between components.
The developer bypassed the pipeline using admin privileges.
Integration tests are too slow for CI. They should run nightly instead.
The branch protection rules are misconfigured — the pipeline ran but wasn't required for merge.

questions.explanation: CI test gates should include multiple test levels — unit tests alone miss integration issues. Add integration tests as required pipeline stages before merge.

questions.question: A test fails in CI but passes when a developer reruns the pipeline. This happens 2-3 times per week. The team starts clicking 'rerun' automatically. What's the danger?

options.text: Higher CI costs — each rerun consumes compute resources.

Trust erosion — the team stops taking failures seriously. When a real bug causes a failure, it gets rerun and merged. Flaky tests become a pipeline tax that reduces the team's ability to catch actual regressions.
Git history pollution — reruns create extra commits in the history.
No real danger — if the test eventually passes, the code is correct. Flaky tests are a normal part of CI.

questions.explanation: Habitual rerunning erodes pipeline trust — when developers reflexively ignore failures, real bugs slip through because they look indistinguishable from flaky test noise.

questions.question: Your CI pipeline runs 3 test stages sequentially: unit (5 min), integration (10 min), e2e (15 min). Total: 30 minutes. The stages are independent. How can you reduce total time?

options.text: Run all three stages in parallel. Since they're independent, they can execute simultaneously, reducing total time from 30 minutes to ~15 minutes (the longest stage).
Remove the longest stage — 15 minutes for e2e is too slow for CI.
Combine all three into one stage — fewer stages means less overhead between them.
Run only unit tests on PRs and save integration + e2e for nightly builds.

questions.explanation: Independent stages should run in parallel — total pipeline time equals the duration of the longest parallel stage, not the sum of all stages.

questions.question: Your CI pipeline for a monorepo runs all tests on every push, even when only a README was changed. Every push triggers a 20-minute pipeline. How do you optimize?

options.text: Use path-based filtering — configure the pipeline to skip test stages when only non-code files (docs, README, .md files) are changed. Run tests only when source code or test files are modified.
Reduce test suite size so even unnecessary runs finish quickly.
Add a manual trigger — developers decide when to run the pipeline.
Queue pipelines and batch pushes — run one pipeline for multiple pushes to reduce total runs.

questions.explanation: Path-based pipeline filtering skips unnecessary work — documentation changes don't need test execution, saving CI resources and reducing developer wait times.

questions.question: Your CI pipeline has a test coverage gate: merge is blocked if coverage drops below 80%. A developer adds a new feature with no tests, but the overall coverage is 81%. The gate passes. What's the flaw?

options.text: The gate checks absolute coverage, not differential coverage. It should also require new code to meet a minimum coverage threshold — otherwise large codebases dilute the impact of untested new code.
80% is too low. Set the threshold to 95% to catch untested features.
Coverage gates shouldn't exist — they incentivize writing low-quality tests just to hit the number.
The coverage tool is miscounting — new code without tests can't maintain 81% overall coverage.

questions.explanation: Absolute coverage gates miss untested new code in large codebases — differential coverage (coverage of changed lines) ensures new code is tested regardless of overall percentage.

questions.question: You have 10 known flaky tests out of 500. The team wants to quarantine them (move to a separate non-blocking stage). What's the right quarantine strategy?

options.text: Delete them — if they can't be trusted, they add no value.
Move flaky tests to a separate non-blocking stage that still runs and reports results. Track them with tickets, set a fix deadline, and automatically promote fixed tests back to the main gate.
Keep them in the main pipeline with auto-retry (3 attempts). If they pass once in 3 tries, count as pass.
Mark them as @skip. Re-enable them after the next major refactor when the underlying code is more stable.

questions.explanation: Quarantine keeps flaky tests visible without blocking the pipeline — combined with fix deadlines and auto-promotion, it prevents quarantine from becoming a graveyard.

questions.question: You split 200 tests across 4 parallel runners (50 each). Runner 1 finishes in 5 minutes, runners 2-4 finish in 15 minutes. What's the problem?

options.text: Uneven distribution — Runner 1 got the fast tests. Split by execution time (weighted balancing), not count, so each runner has approximately equal total execution time.
Runner 1 has better hardware. Ensure all runners use identical machine types.
50 tests per runner is too few. Use 2 runners with 100 tests each for better utilization.
The fast tests on Runner 1 are probably not testing anything meaningful. Investigate their quality.

questions.explanation: Parallel test splitting should balance by execution time, not test count — a few slow tests can dominate one shard while others finish early and sit idle.

questions.question: Your CI pipeline stores test artifacts (screenshots, logs) as pipeline artifacts. After 3 months, the CI storage is full. What's the sustainable solution?

options.text: Delete all artifacts — they're only useful during active debugging.
Set artifact retention policies — keep recent artifacts (7-14 days) automatically and archive critical ones (release builds, failure evidence) to external storage (S3/GCS) with lifecycle rules.
Increase storage — CI platforms offer unlimited storage with enterprise plans.
Stop generating artifacts — logs and screenshots should only be viewed in real-time during pipeline execution.

questions.explanation: Artifact retention policies balance debuggability and storage costs — auto-expire routine artifacts, archive release-critical ones to cheaper storage with lifecycle rules.

questions.question: Your pipeline has a deployment gate that requires manual QA approval. Deployments wait an average of 4 hours for approval because QA is in a different timezone. How do you reduce this without removing the gate?

options.text: Implement automated smoke tests and canary deployments that replace the manual gate. If automated checks pass and canary metrics are healthy for a defined period, auto-promote. Reserve manual approval for high-risk changes using a risk classification system.
Hire QA in the same timezone as the deployment pipeline.
Give developers approval privileges — they can approve their own deployments after self-testing.
Schedule all deployments during QA's business hours only.

questions.explanation: Automated quality gates with risk-based manual approval maximize deployment velocity — low-risk changes flow automatically while high-risk changes get human review.

questions.question: Your team analyzed 6 months of CI data and found that 15% of pipeline failures are caused by flaky tests. The average developer reruns the pipeline 1.5 times per PR. With 50 PRs/week and a 20-minute pipeline, how much developer time is wasted weekly?

options.text: 50 PRs x 0.5 extra runs x 20 minutes = ~8 hours/week of wasted pipeline time. But the real cost includes context-switching, delayed reviews, reduced trust in CI, and the compound effect of developers skipping pipeline results — estimated 2-3x the raw compute time.
Minimal — 20 minutes of automated pipeline time doesn't waste developer time since they work on other things.
50 x 1.5 x 20 = 25 hours of compute time, but developers aren't blocked since they can merge anyway.
The cost is purely financial (CI compute). Developer time isn't affected because pipelines run asynchronously.

questions.explanation: Flaky test costs extend beyond raw compute — context switching, delayed merges, and eroded pipeline trust multiply the impact far beyond the rerun time itself.

questions.question: You run e2e tests across 10 parallel containers against a shared staging database. Tests intermittently fail with 'unique constraint violation' or stale data. Local runs never fail. What's the root cause?

options.text: Parallel tests create conflicting data — multiple containers inserting the same test users, orders, or records simultaneously. Each container needs isolated data (unique identifiers per shard) or its own database instance to prevent cross-contamination.
The staging database can't handle 10 concurrent connections. Connection pool exhaustion causes data corruption.
Containers share a filesystem cache that causes stale reads between test runs.
The database replication lag between read replicas causes tests on different containers to see inconsistent data.

questions.explanation: Parallel tests against shared databases create data conflicts — use per-shard unique test data, separate schemas, or isolated database instances per parallel runner.
